facts.label: COLORS BY DECREE
facts.story: Iraq's identity has been green, white and black since the earliest days of the national team, but none of that mattered in Mexico. Federation president Uday Hussein, son of Saddam, overruled his own delegation and ordered the squad into yellow against Paraguay and pale blue against Belgium and Mexico. Players later said they had pushed to keep the traditional colors and lost the argument. Iraq made its World Cup debut in borrowed hues, then quietly returned to green and white the moment the tournament was over.

facts.label: TEN MEN, ONE GOAL
facts.story: Down a man and down two goals to Belgium in Toluca, Iraq had every reason to fold. Instead, in the 59th minute, Ahmed Radhi met the ball and buried it, the first goal any Iraqi had ever scored at a World Cup. It didn't change the result. Belgium held on to win 2-1. But it made Radhi a permanent line in his country's football history, an answer that will exist for as long as the tournament keeps records.

1986.headline: Ten Men, One Goal

annotations.label: IRAQ'S FIRST EVER GOAL
annotations.story: Playing a man down against Belgium in Toluca, Ahmed Radhi struck in the 59th minute for Iraq's first goal in World Cup history. Belgium still won 2-1, completing Iraq's group-stage exit, but the goal stood as the country's lone highlight from its tournament debut.

annotations.label: A CONTROVERSIAL OPENER
annotations.story: In their opening match, Iraq played Paraguay close and had what they believed was a legitimate goal ruled out by the referee. Paraguay won 1-0 on Julio César Romero's 35th-minute strike, denying Iraq a point in their very first World Cup appearance.

annotations.label: A BRAZILIAN IN CHARGE
annotations.story: Iraq's World Cup debut was steered by Evaristo de Macedo, a Brazilian who'd managed his own national team just a year earlier, now drilling Iraqi players into a disciplined defensive shape. He built the side while the Iran-Iraq War ground into its sixth year back home — and decades later, Iraq still hasn't returned to a World Cup.

1986.homeColors: yellow shirt with black trim on the collar, shoulder stripes and sleeve cuffs (Adidas)
1986.awayColors: light blue shirt with white V-neck collar and Adidas three stripes on the shoulders

2026.headline: Forty Years, Same Result
2026.homeColors: white shirt with interlocking diamonds and circular motifs inspired by the mosaics of Uruk, red-white-black-navy trim on collar and cuffs (Jako)
2026.awayColors: deep green shirt repeating the Uruk mosaic pattern in a darker tonal register (Jako)

annotations.label: THE KEY MOMENT
annotations.story: In Boston, Erling Haaland scored twice on his World Cup debut to sink Iraq's opener, doubling Norway's lead before halftime. Aymen Hussein pulled one back in the 39th minute, briefly making it 2–1, but a late own goal sealed a 4–1 defeat that set the tone for a winless return to the tournament.

annotations.label: THE KIT
annotations.story: German manufacturer Jako built Iraq's white home shirt around interlocking diamonds and circular motifs lifted from the ancient mosaics of Uruk, the Mesopotamian city credited as the birthplace of writing. Collar and cuff stripes carried the flag's red, white, black and navy, while a deep green away kit repeated the pattern in a darker register.

annotations.label: THE CONTEXT
annotations.story: Iraq's return came forty years after its only previous appearance, sealed with a 2–1 play-off win over Bolivia in Monterrey — the same country that hosted their 1986 debut. Ali al-Hamadi and Aymen Hussein scored the goals that ended the country's longest active World Cup drought and sent the Lions of Mesopotamia back to the tournament.
